Long ago, before the mountains had names, the sky was a heavy blanket of gray wool that smothered the world. The people walked hunched over, their eyes cast downward, for there was no light to guide their gaze upward. They knew only the damp earth and the cold wind.

One day, a young weaver named Elara grew tired of the gloom. She climbed the tallest peak, carrying her golden spindle and a bag of glowing embers she had stolen from the belly of a volcano. At the summit, where the woolly sky scraped the rock, she began to spin. She mixed the volcanic fire with the gray threads of the sky, weaving furiously as the cold wind bit at her fingers.

As she worked, the friction of her golden spindle sparked against the heavy clouds. First came a single star, a tiny pinprick of hope. Then came the moon, a curved slice of her silver shears. Finally, exhausted but triumphant, she tore the heavy gray wool apart with a mighty shout. The embers scattered, becoming the sun and the galaxies. The heavy blanket dissolved into wisps of clouds, lifting high above the earth. The people stood tall for the first time, their faces bathed in golden light, and they named the sun after the weaver's burning heart.

Long ago, before the mountains had roots, the sky was a heavy blanket of indigo velvet that rested directly upon the earth. The people walked hunched over, unable to stand tall, whispering their dreams into the dust. Among them lived a weaver named Elara, who possessed a needle carved from the first ray of dawn.

Tired of the darkness, Elara climbed the highest hill, her back scraping against the stars. She began to sew. With her glowing needle, she pierced the indigo fabric, stitching a pattern of silver thread that she pulled from her own grey hair. As she pulled the thread taut, the sky began to lift.

Inch by inch, the heavy velvet rose. The people stood straighter, gasping as fresh wind rushed into the new space. Elara stitched faster, creating constellations of hope—the Bear to guard them, the Archer to hunt for them. But the sky grew heavy again, threatening to fall.

With one final, desperate thrust, Elara pushed her needle through the fabric and pinned it to the highest point of the universe. The needle shattered, exploding into the sun. The sky held firm, high and blue, but Elara vanished into the light, forever weaving the clouds to remind us that even the heaviest burdens can be lifted by a single thread of courage.

With every stroke of the feather, she pushed the fabric upward, coloring it with the azure of her longing and the white of her breath. The sky resisted, heavy and wet, but Elara painted faster, her strokes becoming wind, her sweat becoming rain.

As the dome rose, creating the vault of the heavens, the friction of the dream-feather against the rough fabric ignited. It burst into a sphere of blinding gold—the sun. The sky locked into place, high and unreachable, and Elara was consumed by her own creation, becoming the first wind to ever roam the open world, forever pushing the clouds to keep the ceiling from falling.

Myth: In the epoch of the Stone Canopy, the heavens were a slab of granite crushing the world. Men and women moved like beetles in the dark, their chests scraping the dirt. Elara, a keeper of the dead soil, found a single, pulsing ember buried in the cold clay—a seed of pure fire left from a forgotten age. While others sought to chip away at the stone, Elara chose to nurture the spark.

She buried the ember at the world's navel and watered it with oil and her own warm breath. Overnight, a vine of blinding plasma erupted from the ground. It did not grow wood or leaf, but loops of searing light. The vine lashed out, striking the granite ceiling and driving it upward with the force of boiling heat. The stone groaned and retreated, melting under the assault.

The molten rock gathered at the vine’s apex, coalescing into the burning sphere of the sun. The vine itself solidified into the golden axis upon which the world now spins. To prevent the fire from consuming the earth it had saved, Elara dissolved into the cool morning mist, forever rising to soothe the scorched ground and temper the heat of her wild, bright garden.
